text:
`git reset --soft HEAD~1`

That undoes the last commit while leaving all its changes staged, exactly as they were just before you committed. Your working tree is untouched.

A couple of related variants in case you want something slightly different:

- `git reset HEAD~1` (mixed, the default) — undoes the commit and unstages the changes, so they stay in your working tree but you'd need to `git add` again.
- `git reset --hard HEAD~1` — undoes the commit and discards the changes entirely. Destructive, so only use it if you're sure.

If the commit was already pushed, undoing it rewrites history, so you'd need a force-push (`git push --force-with-lease`) and should coordinate with anyone else on the branch.

promptText: How do I undo my last git commit but keep the changes staged?
